artifacts.name: Kandyan Sword (Kasthāne)
artifacts.materials: Forged steel blade; brass/silver hilt; wood; gemstone inlays
artifacts.function: Used in royal ceremonies, investitures, and as visible rank insignia. Carried by nobles during processions and occasionally used in ritualized demonstrations of martial skill; also part of diplomatic gift exchanges.
artifacts.symbolism: Embodies royal authority, protector role of the king, and the martial lineage of Kandyan aristocracy. The Makara/dragon motifs mark protective power and link to mythical guardians.
artifacts.notes: Hilt often shaped like a Makara (mythical sea-creature). Many examples show inlay work, ceremonial scabbards, and are inscribed or gifted as diplomacy tokens; some blades were intentionally blunted for ceremonial use.

artifacts.name: Japanese Katana
artifacts.materials: Folded tamahagane steel; ray-skin; silk cord
artifacts.function: Primary battlefield and dueling weapon historically; later a status and ritual item. Used in paired sets (daisho) and in ceremonial rites like seppuku/installation rituals; sometimes mounted at shrines.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents bushidō values (honor, loyalty, discipline). Considered an extension of the warrior’s identity — “soul of the samurai” — with ritualized care, mounting, and presentation.
artifacts.notes: Forged with folding and differential hardening (hamon). Tsuba (guard) often artistically decorated; many katanas have provenance records and smith signatures (mei).

artifacts.name: Geta Bera (Kandyan Drum)
artifacts.materials: Jackfruit wood body; cow/monkey hide heads; rope braces
artifacts.function: Provides the rhythmic backbone of Kandyan dance forms and ritual processions. Sets tempo for dancers, signals choreographic changes, and accompanies chant and percussion ensembles in temple ceremonies.
artifacts.symbolism: Signifies sacred beat linking community and deity; rhythmic patterns mark ritual phases and solemnity, and the drum’s sound is considered to call or please protective spirits.
artifacts.location: Kandy region temples; cultural dance schools
artifacts.notes: Double-headed construction tuned asymmetrically for tonal contrast. Drummers often undergo training that includes ritual purification; sticks and playing techniques are specific to each dance type (Ves, Naiyandi).

artifacts.name: Japanese Taiko Drum
artifacts.dimensions: Small hand drums up to 1–2 m
artifacts.materials: Hollowed zelkova or keyaki wood; animal hide
artifacts.function: Used for religious processions, ensemble performance (kumi-daiko), theatrical accompaniment (Noh/ Kabuki), and community signaling. In modern performance, taiko creates dramatic, choreographed ensemble shows.
artifacts.symbolism: Expresses communal cohesion, ritual presence, and the visceral power of sound; large taiko embody public energy and sacred force in festivals.
artifacts.notes: Variety of sizes; some taiko are portable for parades, others are massive and stationary. Contemporary groups (e.g., Kodo) have revived and globalized taiko performance.

artifacts.name: Kandyan Temple Mural
artifacts.materials: Lime plaster; mineral pigments; organic binders; occasional gold leaf
artifacts.function: Serves as didactic visual scripture: instructs lay patrons in Jataka tales and ethical precepts, supports liturgy, and visually anchors devotional space for worship. Murals also expressed royal patronage.
artifacts.symbolism: Symbolizes moral order, karmic consequences, and the sanctity of the monastic community; murals encode cosmology and ethical exemplars for believers.
artifacts.notes: Restricted palette (reds, yellows, blacks) and flattened perspective; pigments derived from local minerals and organic binders cause particular conservation needs. Many murals have layered retouching histories tied to temple restorations.

artifacts.name: Thai Temple Mural
artifacts.materials: Mineral pigments, gold leaf, plaster
artifacts.function: Narrates epic cycles and Buddhist instruction for devotees; murals are integrated with ritual procession routes and icon display. Commissioned by royal or monastic patrons for instruction and prestige.
artifacts.symbolism: Conveys royal religious legitimacy, cosmic order, and idealized moral conduct; gold leaf raises sacredness and signals elite sponsorship.
artifacts.notes: Highly detailed narrative registers, rich color palette and gold highlight; murals often reflect courtly aesthetics and include donor portraits.

artifacts.name: Sri Lankan Clay Pot
artifacts.materials: Local red/yellow clay; sand or organic temper
artifacts.function: Everyday vessel for cooking (esp. rice), water cooling and storage, fermenting foods, and sometimes ritual libations. Form and surface finish reflect function (pouring spouts, rims for lids).
artifacts.symbolism: Symbolic of domestic continuity, hospitality, and elemental connection to earth; pot forms are integrated into ritual food offerings and seasonal cycles.
artifacts.notes: Unglazed porous bodies allow evaporative cooling; traditional pots are made by coiling or wheel techniques and may be fired in open kilns. Decorative motifs vary regionally.

artifacts.name: Egyptian Amphora
artifacts.materials: Terracotta; slip coatings and painted pigments
artifacts.function: Long-distance transport containers for oil, wine, salted fish and grains; used in domestic storage and as grave goods. Shapes relate to shipping and storage efficiency.
artifacts.symbolism: Emblem of trade networks, wealth, and civic provisioning; decorated amphora often reflect mythological or civic identity.
artifacts.location: Archaeological sites in the Nile Delta; museums (e.g., British Museum)
artifacts.notes: Biconical bodies, twin handles and narrow necks optimized for stowage and pouring; painted or stamped indices can indicate contents or makers.

artifacts.name: Kolam Mask
artifacts.materials: Kaduru (native hardwood), plant pigments, lacquer
artifacts.function: Central prop for Kolam performances — masks animate characters in satirical plays that comment on village life, social norms and morality. Also used in healing/demonstrative rites.
artifacts.symbolism: Embodies social critique and cultural memory; masks play dual roles as entertainment and social correction, often encoding local cosmologies.
artifacts.notes: Masks are carved to specific character types (villagers, demons, kings). Painted colors indicate temperament; lightweight woods chosen for mobility during dance.

artifacts.name: African Ritual Mask
artifacts.materials: Local hardwoods, natural pigments, beads, raffia
artifacts.function: Worn during initiation ceremonies, harvest rites, funerary and royal performances; often integrated with dance, music, and sacrifice. Masks activate social and spiritual functions.
artifacts.symbolism: Serve as intermediaries to ancestors and spirits; they mediate community identity, moral law, and fertility/protection rites.
artifacts.notes: Often part of a full costume ensemble; specific masks are restricted to initiated members and may be ritually charged (consecrated).

artifacts.name: Laksha Lacquerware
artifacts.materials: Seasoned wood, lac (natural resin), plant dyes
artifacts.function: Functional as containers for ritual substances, personal ornaments, or processional items; also used as decorative gifts and temple offerings.
artifacts.symbolism: Symbolizes refinement, prosperity, and continuity of handcraft lineage; lacquer sheen signals protection and value.
artifacts.notes: Made on lathes with successive lacquer layers rubbed to polish; patterns often use circular/spiral motifs; dyes come from local plant sources.

artifacts.name: Channapatna Lacquerware
artifacts.materials: Turned wood (woolly-wood), lacquer, natural dyes
artifacts.function: Domestic decorative objects and children’s toys; historically trade items.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents community-level artisan systems and regional identity; brightly colored forms connote cheerful domesticity.
artifacts.notes: Recognized with GI status; natural dyes and lacquering techniques are preserved through craft schools.

artifacts.name: Brass Oil Lamp (Pahana)
artifacts.materials: Brass/bronze alloys
artifacts.function: Lit in temples, shrines and domestic altars during puja, processions and festivals; also used to inaugurate events and mark auspicious starts.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents dispelling of ignorance by light; lamp-lighting is both devotional act and social ritual signalling hospitality and blessing.
artifacts.notes: Often shaped with culturally resonant motifs (peacock, elephant). Permits multiple wicks; sometimes carried on processional poles.

artifacts.name: Indian Deepam Lamp
artifacts.materials: Bronze/brass
artifacts.function: Central in Hindu puja practice: lit before deities, used in arati and festival observances (e.g., Deepavali).
artifacts.symbolism: Symbolizes divine presence, prosperity and grace; light connects mundane to divine.
artifacts.notes: Lamps often form part of ritual paraphernalia set; some are richly ornamented with iconography of Lakshmi or Shiva.

artifacts.name: Beeralu Lace (Bobbin Lace)
artifacts.materials: Cotton thread, wooden bobbins
artifacts.function: Used to embellish garments, religious textiles, and household linens; lace-making is a domestic craft that supported women’s incomes and social networks.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents fine handwork, female artistic labor, and cultural exchange (Portuguese influence).
artifacts.notes: Design complexity varies by locale; tools include lace pillows and pins. Some patterns are unique family motifs preserved across generations.

artifacts.name: Belgian Bobbin Lace
artifacts.materials: Linen, silk, sometimes metallic threads
artifacts.function: High-status clothing, ecclesiastical vestments, ceremonial linens.
artifacts.symbolism: Signifies aristocratic taste, refinement and luxury craftsmanship.
artifacts.notes: Technique produces extremely fine openwork; lace patterns may identify region or workshop.

artifacts.name: Kurahan Gala / Miris Gala (Grinding Stone)
artifacts.materials: Granite
artifacts.function: Primary household implement for milling grains (kurakkan), spices and chilies; often used daily in food preparation and ritual food production.
artifacts.symbolism: Symbol of domestic continuity, female labor and food sovereignty; often inherits familial significance.
artifacts.notes: Hand-operated method imparts distinctive texture and flavor. Stone often kept as family heirloom with care.

artifacts.name: Mayan Metate
artifacts.materials: Basalt/volcanic stone
artifacts.function: Grinding maize and other staples for daily meals (tortillas), and in ritual contexts for offering preparations.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents female domestic role, sustenance, and cultural continuity; some metates have ritualized designs.
artifacts.notes: Often anthropomorphized in ceremonial pieces. Design and use persist in many communities.

artifacts.name: Sandakada Pahana (Moonstone)
artifacts.materials: Granite
artifacts.function: Placed at temple thresholds to mark and sanctify the transition from profane to sacred space; also tied to ritual movement during processions.
artifacts.symbolism: Embodies cosmological cycles (samsara), purity and progression toward liberation; animals and floral bands encode moral and cosmological meanings.
artifacts.notes: Carving follows a consistent banded program: outer flames, animals, creepers and central lotus. Moonstones are diagnostic of Sri Lankan Buddhist architecture.

artifacts.name: Indian Temple Threshold Stone
artifacts.materials: Granite or sandstone
artifacts.function: Serves as entrance feature for temples, often used in ritual circumambulation and marking auspicious thresholds.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents purification, auspiciousness and protection; threshold carvings often include deities, lotus and guardian motifs.
artifacts.notes: Thresholds often integrated with carved doorframes (toranas) and elaborate lintels; iconography may vary by sectarian association (Shaiva/Vaishnava).

artifacts.name: Punkalasa (Pot of Plenty)
artifacts.materials: Terracotta or carved stone
artifacts.function: Used as votive motif or temple ornament symbolizing abundance and invoked in harvest/royal rituals; sometimes present in temple iconography or processions.
artifacts.symbolism: Emblems of fertility, prosperity, and royal largesse — pot overflowing with produce denotes blessing and sustenance.
artifacts.notes: Often sculpted as part of temple friezes or freestanding vessels; may be painted or gilded in sacred contexts.

artifacts.name: Greek Cornucopia (Horn of Plenty)
artifacts.materials: Clay, bronze, marble
artifacts.function: Used as votive and decorative motif in art and ritual scenes celebrating harvest and abundance.
artifacts.symbolism: Classical emblem of prosperity tied to agricultural gods (e.g., Demeter) and civic wellbeing.
artifacts.notes: Often depicted overflowing with fruit and grain; appears in funerary and domestic contexts.

artifacts.name: Wangediya Mortar (Granite mortar)
artifacts.materials: Granite
artifacts.function: Used to grind herbs, spices and prepare Ayurvedic pastes; essential in household and medicinal preparations.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents healing knowledge and domestic food technology passed through families.
artifacts.notes: Often heavy and durable; sometimes inscribed with maker marks; paired with wooden pestle (mol gaha).

artifacts.name: Japanese Suribachi Mortar
artifacts.materials: Ceramic with internal ridges
artifacts.function: Ideal for grinding sesame, spice blends and making sauces/condiments; kitchen staple for fine emulsification.
artifacts.symbolism: Embodies culinary refinement and ritualized food preparation in household kitchens.
artifacts.notes: Interior combed ridges (kushi) increase grinding efficiency; typically used with wooden pestle (surikogi).

artifacts.name: Sil Batta (Indian Stone Mortar)
artifacts.materials: Flat volcanic or granite stone; stone pestle
artifacts.function: Primary grinding tool for daily food preparation; used for grinding coconut, spices, rice, and preparing chutneys and pastes in South Indian cuisines.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents domestic continuity and the foundation of culinary traditions; grinding is considered meditative and ritualistic in food preparation.
artifacts.location: South Indian households; regional cooking schools
artifacts.notes: Stone selection is crucial for efficiency; some families maintain heirloom mortars passed through generations. Distinct from mortar-pestle sets; emphasizes horizontal grinding motion.

artifacts.name: Thai Granite Mortar (Kratip)
artifacts.materials: Granite or limestone; wooden pestle
artifacts.function: Essential tool for preparing Thai seasoning pastes (nam prik, curry pastes); used for crushing herbs, garlic, chilies, and creating complex flavor bases.
artifacts.symbolism: Central to Thai culinary identity; the ritual of grinding aromatics is considered foundational to authentic Thai cooking technique.
artifacts.notes: Distinctive deep, wide bowl accommodates ingredients; wooden pestle prevents fragmentation of aromatics. Often decorated with traditional patterns.

artifacts.name: Molcajete (Mexican Volcanic Stone Mortar)
artifacts.materials: Porous volcanic stone (basalt); stone pestle (tejolote)
artifacts.function: Traditional tool for grinding and mixing ingredients for salsas, guacamole, and medicinal preparations; also used for crushing spices and corn.
artifacts.symbolism: Ancient Aztec technology; represents indigenous culinary knowledge and spiritual connection to earth (stone symbolizes earth element in Mesoamerican cosmology).
artifacts.notes: Three short legs support the bowl and distribute heat; porous stone texture essential for grinding; newer molcajetes are seasoned by grinding rice or seeds over time.

artifacts.name: Kandyan Silver Jewelry (Nalalpati, Earrings)
artifacts.materials: Silver, gold plating, semi-precious stones
artifacts.function: Worn for status display in weddings, temple offerings, dance costumes and as family heirlooms; used to identify caste/region in some contexts.
artifacts.symbolism: Signals wealth, ritual readiness, and regional identity; often linked to temple donation practices.
artifacts.location: Temple treasuries; private collections in Kandy
artifacts.notes: Features filigree, granulation, and repoussé techniques; jewelry documents trade ties via gem materials and stylistic influences.

artifacts.name: Mughal Silver Jewelry
artifacts.materials: Silver, gold, pearls, gemstones
artifacts.function: Designed for court display, ceremonial dress, and as diplomatic gifts; often part of imperial treasure.
artifacts.symbolism: Expresses imperial power, cosmopolitan taste (Persian influence) and devotional patronage.
artifacts.notes: Intricate stone settings, enamel work (meenakari) in some pieces; provenance can often be traced through court records.

artifacts.name: Wooden Pillar Carvings (Embekke-style)
artifacts.materials: Jack, teak or local hardwood
artifacts.function: Structural posts for temple halls and palace pavilions that also carry narrative and decorative carving; integrate iconography with structural function.
artifacts.symbolism: Signify royal patronage, devotional stories, and skilled craft lineage; carved motifs may include protective and auspicious figures.
artifacts.location: Embekke Devalaya and other historic sites
artifacts.notes: Extremely high relief carving with dancers, wrestlers, floral patterns; joinery techniques avoid metal fasteners in some examples.

artifacts.name: Balinese Temple Wood Carvings
artifacts.materials: Teak, jack or local hardwood
artifacts.function: Decorate temple gateways, shrines and domestic shrines; act as narrative surfaces and protective talismans.
artifacts.symbolism: Represent mythic stories (Ramayana/Mahabharata), guardian beings and auspicious symbols that protect the temple precinct.
artifacts.notes: Often painted/gilded; high relief with swirling vegetal patterns and animated guardian figures; carved by specialized families.

artifacts.name: Brass Betel Cutter (Bulath Kaduwa)
artifacts.materials: Brass, iron
artifacts.function: Used during betel-chewing ceremonies to cut areca nut leaves and prepare betel offerings for guests and during rituals like weddings.
artifacts.symbolism: Signifies hospitality, reciprocal social bonds, and ritual courtesy; betel sets are important in social negotiation and ceremony.
artifacts.notes: Designs often stylized (birds, fish, religious motifs); part of a set that includes betel tray, lime box and nut pick.

artifacts.name: Chinese / SE Asian Areca Nut Cutter
artifacts.materials: Bronze, iron
artifacts.function: Used to slice areca nut for betel-chewing in social and ritual contexts; tool carried in social sets.
artifacts.symbolism: Emphasizes hospitality, social reciprocity, and ritualized daily practice across many Asian cultures.
artifacts.notes: Often engraved with dragons or auspicious emblems; used in both secular and ceremonial contexts.

artifacts.name: Rajput Talwar
artifacts.materials: Forged high-carbon steel blade; brass/gold hilt; leather-wrapped grip; precious stone inlays
artifacts.function: Primary weapon of nobility and warriors; used in battle and on horseback; also served as ceremonial regalia in courts and processions.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents martial prowess, royal authority, and honor codes of Rajput chivalry; hilt often decorated with auspicious and protective motifs.
artifacts.location: Indian palace museums; private collections in Jaipur and Udaipur
artifacts.notes: Curved steel blade optimized for cavalry use; distinctive crossguard (quillons) often features animal heads. Grip weight allows efficient sweeping motions.

artifacts.name: Ottoman Scimitar (Sabre)
artifacts.materials: Damascus steel blade; ivory/bone hilt; gold and silver inlay
artifacts.function: Elite weapon for Janissary troops and Ottoman nobility; used in military campaigns and court ceremonies; symbol of imperial authority.
artifacts.symbolism: Embodies Ottoman power, military tradition, and cosmopolitan empire; curved blade reflects Islamic geometric aesthetics and Central Asian martial tradition.
artifacts.location: Istanbul Topkapi Palace collections; European museums
artifacts.notes: Highly curved blade allows slashing efficiency; often decorated with Quranic inscriptions and geometric patterns; many have documented provenance through sultanic collections.

artifacts.name: Persian Khanda (Sword)
artifacts.materials: Steel blade; brass/bronze hilt; semi-precious stone inlays; silk wrapping
artifacts.function: Worn by nobility and military commanders; used in royal ceremonies, investitures, and courtly displays of wealth and power.
artifacts.symbolism: Signifies Safavid imperial might, refined aesthetics, and connection to pre-Islamic Persian warrior tradition; blade often carries proverbs or royal emblems.
artifacts.notes: Straight or slightly curved blade; distinctive crossguard; hilt work often rivals contemporary European jeweled hilts. Many examples show Persian calligraphic script.

artifacts.name: Mridangam (Classical Drum)
artifacts.materials: Wooden body (jackfruit or mango); animal hide heads; rope tuning system
artifacts.function: Primary percussion instrument in Bharata Natyam classical dance and Carnatic music; sets tempo and marks rhythmic phrases (talas) integral to performance.
artifacts.symbolism: Sacred instrument embodying the divine pulse; rhythm is considered manifestation of cosmic order (Natya Shastra principles).
artifacts.location: South Indian temples; classical music schools and concert halls
artifacts.notes: Played with hands and fingers; distinctive two-headed construction tuned to complimentary pitches. Complex system of solkattu (syllabic notation) directs performance.

artifacts.name: African Talking Drum (Yoruba Dun Dun)
artifacts.materials: Wooden barrel body; animal hide; rope or cord braces
artifacts.function: Used to transmit messages through drum language that mimics tonal patterns of Yoruba speech; also accompanies ritual ceremonies and social gatherings.
artifacts.symbolism: Embodies communication between human and spirit worlds; drum 'speech' carries ancestral knowledge and social announcements.
artifacts.notes: Pressure applied to rope braces changes pitch; skilled drummers can convey complex messages and poetry through coded rhythmic patterns.

artifacts.name: Arabian Naqqara Drum
artifacts.materials: Copper or bronze hemispheric bowl; animal hide; wooden or metal beaters
artifacts.function: Played in royal courts, military processions, and ceremonial celebrations; pairs of naqqaras provide the foundational rhythm for ensemble performance.
artifacts.symbolism: Symbol of imperial power and courtly sophistication; distinctive metallic tone associated with formal occasions and diplomatic ceremonies.
artifacts.location: Islamic art museums; private collections in Gulf states
artifacts.notes: Often hung from ornate frames; produces deep, resonant tones used in festive and martial contexts. Sound carries particular prestige in celebratory events.

artifacts.name: Celadon Porcelain Vessel
artifacts.materials: Porcelain; celadon glaze (jade-green color from iron oxide)
artifacts.function: Prestigious tableware for noble courts; used in ceremonial contexts; highly valued in trade routes to Islamic and European courts.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents refined aesthetics, imperial patronage, and sophisticated technology; jade-green glaze symbolic of immortality and natural harmony.
artifacts.notes: Crackle glaze and subtle forms reflect literati aesthetics; some vessels bear sung imperial marks. Greatly desired across trade networks.

artifacts.name: Greek Kylix (Drinking Cup)
artifacts.materials: Terracotta; red-figure or black-figure decoration
artifacts.function: Used in symposium gatherings for wine drinking; often displayed in honor of guests; important vessel in elite male social rituals.
artifacts.symbolism: Emblem of symposium culture, philosophical discourse, and aristocratic refinement; decorated figures narrate myths and social values.
artifacts.notes: Wide opening and shallow bowl optimized for communal drinking; interior often decorated with portrait medallions or mythological scenes that reveal themselves as cup empties.

artifacts.name: Islamic Geometric Ceramic Bowl
artifacts.materials: Composite ceramic; underglaze painted decoration; turquoise and cobalt blue glazes
artifacts.function: Decorative tableware for elite households; used in courtly dining and display; reflects patron's refined taste and cultural sophistication.
artifacts.symbolism: Intricate geometric and floral patterns embodying Islamic mathematical harmony and spiritual geometry; often inscribed with poetry or blessings.
artifacts.notes: Complex polychrome decoration under clear glaze; some examples feature complex calligraphy. These vessels were highly valued trade items.

artifacts.name: Thai Khon Mask
artifacts.materials: Lacquered papier-mâché or wood; gold leaf; semi-precious stone inlays; horsehair
artifacts.function: Central to Khon classical dance-drama performances narrating the Ramakien; wore by solo male performers; part of royal court entertainment.
artifacts.symbolism: Defines character and spiritual essence; elaborate gold decoration signals high rank and divine or noble status; different mask colors encode character temperament.
artifacts.location: Thai National Museum; royal palace collections
artifacts.notes: Highly stylized with exaggerated features; different mask types for gods, demons, humans; worn with elaborate costume and choreographed movement.

artifacts.name: Venetian Carnival Mask (Volto)
artifacts.materials: Paper-mâché; paint; gold and silver leaf; sometimes feathers or jewels
artifacts.function: Worn during Carnival season to allow temporary social anonymity and role reversal; worn in theaters and public gatherings.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents liberation from social hierarchy; mask-wearing permits social critique and playful transgression; tradition dates to medieval mystery plays.
artifacts.location: Venetian museums; private collections across Europe
artifacts.notes: Range of styles (Volto, Bauta, Larva); elaborate decorated versions signify wealth; simple versions accessible to common citizens.

artifacts.name: Chinese Peking Opera Mask (Lianpu)
artifacts.materials: Rigid lacquered paper or leather; bright mineral pigments; gold leaf
artifacts.function: Worn by male actors in Peking opera; different masks define character type, moral status, and emotional temperament in narrative performance.
artifacts.symbolism: Elaborate color symbolism: red = loyalty/righteousness, black = strength/honesty, white = cunning/evil, blue = boldness; patterns encode character history.
artifacts.location: Beijing Opera museums; private actor collections
artifacts.notes: Complex standardized system with hundreds of documented mask types; patterns indicate age, social rank, supernatural status. Worn with stylized movement system.

artifacts.name: Roman Oil Lamp (Lucerna)
artifacts.materials: Terracotta; sometimes bronze (luxury versions)
artifacts.function: Primary light source in homes and public spaces; filled with olive oil and lit with rush wicks; sometimes carried in public processions.
artifacts.symbolism: Practical necessity and symbol of civilization; oil lamps used in burial contexts symbolize eternal light or journey to afterlife.
artifacts.notes: Often molded with decorative features or maker marks; designs range from simple to elaborate with mythological or gladiatorial scenes embossed on surface.

artifacts.name: Islamic Mosque Hanging Lamp (Mamluk era)
artifacts.materials: Brass/bronze; colored glass insets; intricate metalwork
artifacts.function: Suspended from mosque ceilings to illuminate prayer spaces during evening prayers; also symbolized spiritual illumination; highly valued ritual objects.
artifacts.symbolism: Light represents divine presence and spiritual enlightenment in Islamic theology; elaborate brass work signals patron's piety and wealth.
artifacts.location: Cairo Museum collections; museum of Islamic art
artifacts.notes: Often inlaid with colorful glass creating luminous effect; many bear Quranic inscriptions or calligraphic panels. Each lamp is craftwork of master artisans.

artifacts.name: Chinese Ceramic Lantern
artifacts.materials: Porcelain or ceramic; blue-and-white painted decoration; silk interior
artifacts.function: Used in festivals and celebrations; carried in processions during Lantern Festival; also displayed in palaces and temples during ceremonial occasions.
artifacts.symbolism: Light and illumination represent happiness, reunion, and divine presence; lanterns used in rites to guide souls and celebrate seasonal transitions.
artifacts.location: Chinese palace museums; international ceramic collections
artifacts.notes: Often featuring auspicious symbols (dragons, phoenixes, characters for longevity); some lanterns open to reveal interior scenes or puppet mechanisms.

artifacts.name: Mexican Pre-Columbian Mural
artifacts.materials: Lime plaster; mineral-based pigments; natural binders
artifacts.function: Decorated temple and palace interior spaces; depicted ritual ceremonies, deities, and cosmological narratives; served didactic and spiritual functions.
artifacts.symbolism: Encoded astronomical knowledge, ritual calendar, and mythological narratives; colors and glyphs convey spiritual significance and power hierarchies.
artifacts.location: Mexico City museums; archaeological sites in situ (Teotihuacán, Bonampak)
artifacts.notes: Sophisticated compositional systems; figures often arranged in hierarchical registers. Many murals document pre-Columbian astronomical observations.

artifacts.name: Mughal Fresco / Miniature Painting
artifacts.materials: Paper; mineral pigments (lapis lazuli, malachite, gold leaf); water-based binders
artifacts.function: Decorated imperial manuscripts (Akbarnama, Baburnama); commissioned by emperors for court display and historical records; also created frescoes in palace walls.
artifacts.symbolism: Demonstrates imperial power, cosmopolitan aesthetics blending Persian and Indian traditions; gold leaf signals prestige and sacred patronage.
artifacts.location: Delhi palace collections; Victoria & Albert Museum; Chester Beatty Library
artifacts.notes: Remarkable detail and perspective; often depicts court scenes, hunts, and battles. Technique involves numerous transparent glazes built up through layers.

artifacts.name: Chinese Tang Dynasty Wall Painting
artifacts.dimensions: Several meters across tomb or temple walls
artifacts.materials: Mineral pigments; charcoal; plant-based binders; applied to plaster
artifacts.function: Decorated imperial tombs and temples; depicted celestial scenes, attendants, and courtly life; guided souls in afterlife journey in tomb contexts.
artifacts.symbolism: Represented celestial hierarchies, cosmic order, and immortal aspirations; careful iconography conveyed spiritual status and worldview.
artifacts.location: Xi'an museums (Xi'an History Museum); archaeological collections
artifacts.notes: Sophisticated landscape and perspective techniques; pigments include rare materials imported from Central Asia. Many murals preserve evidence of trade interconnections.

artifacts.name: Kandyan Royal Robes (Kandyan Jacket & Skirt)
artifacts.dimensions: Full-length ensemble; jacket 60–80 cm, skirt 90–120 cm
artifacts.materials: Silk; velvet; brocade; gold braid trim; gemstone buttons; elaborate needlework
artifacts.function: Worn by Kandyan kings and nobility during court ceremonies, state events, and temple rituals. Essential regalia for investiture ceremonies and diplomatic receptions.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents royal authority, divine favor and the continuity of Kandyan sovereignty. The gold embroidery and costly materials signal power and patronage of the arts.
artifacts.location: National Museum, Colombo; Royal Palace Museum, Kandy
artifacts.notes: Typically features elaborate gold threadwork depicting religious motifs and palatial scenes. Many robes were commissioned by royal decree and became heirlooms. The distinctive shape and fastening techniques are uniquely Kandyan.

artifacts.name: Japanese Kimono (Edo/Meiji Period Imperial Robe)
artifacts.materials: Silk; brocade; hand-dyed patterns; embroidered motifs; metal accessories (obi clasps)
artifacts.function: Worn by imperial court members and nobility during ceremonial occasions, festivals, and state entertainments. Used in Ōhayame and court dances.
artifacts.symbolism: Signifies status, refinement, and adherence to court etiquette. Patterns and colors encode seasonal awareness and aesthetic philosophy (wabi-sabi).
artifacts.location: Tokyo National Museum; Kyoto Costume Museum
artifacts.notes: Distinctive features include elaborate yuzen dyeing, gold leaf applications, and family crests (kamon). Construction requires highly specialized tailoring; many imperial robes are registered national treasures.

artifacts.name: Sri Lankan Saree (Kandyan High Saree)
artifacts.materials: Silk; cotton; gold thread; woven with intricate patterns
artifacts.function: Worn by Sri Lankan women for formal occasions, weddings, temple visits, and cultural celebrations. The 'high saree' (distinctive wrapped style) is particularly associated with the Central Highlands.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents cultural identity, feminine grace, and connection to Sri Lankan heritage. The ornate design and gentle draping embody modesty and sophistication.
artifacts.location: Textile museums, Colombo; private collections; artisan weaving centers
artifacts.notes: Distinguished by specific weaving patterns including the famous 'kandyan pattern' with gold accents. Many families maintain heirloom sarees passed through generations; traditional dyeing and weaving methods are still practiced.

artifacts.name: Chinese Imperial Dragon Robe (Longpao)
artifacts.materials: Silk; gold brocade; embroidered dragons; precious metal threads; fur trim
artifacts.function: Ceremonial garment worn exclusively by emperors and imperial family members during state ceremonies, court rituals, and important celebrations.
artifacts.symbolism: The dragon motif embodies imperial power and cosmic authority. The robe's elaborate construction and exclusive use in court contexts signal supreme status and mandate of heaven.
artifacts.location: Palace Museum, Beijing; Metropolitan Museum of Art (NYC)
artifacts.notes: Strictly hierarchical designs with imperial dragon positions and prohibited patterns for lower ranks. Many robes feature complex silk embroidery requiring months of skilled labor. Each dragon has specific iconographic meaning.

artifacts.name: Indian Rajput Royal Attire (Sherwani & Turban)
artifacts.materials: Silk; velvet; gold brocade; precious gemstone embroidery; ostrich plumes (turban)
artifacts.function: Worn by Rajput nobles and warriors during court ceremonies, military campaigns, and state functions. The ensemble signified martial prowess and royal lineage.
artifacts.symbolism: Represents Rajput honor, chivalric values, and pride in martial traditions. The turban's height and ornamentation indicated rank and regional affiliation.
artifacts.location: Museums of Rajasthan (Jaipur, Udaipur); Indian textile museums
artifacts.notes: Distinctive Rajput variations in turban wrapping style and color preferences (red, saffron, gold). Many sherwanis retain intricate pattern work depicting hunting scenes and religious motifs. Ensemble often paired with ornate jewelry and weapons.
